prior.full_brief:
# Market Signal Brief: U.S. Sustainable Packaging Landscape — EPR Expansion, Circular Material Innovation, and Compliance Cost Headwinds

**Format:** Market Signal Brief | **Item Type:** market\_signal, initiative
**Date of Brief:** 2026-06-10

---

# What's Moving and What Triggered It

MONITORING

Three converging forces are reshaping the U.S. sustainable packaging supply chain simultaneously, each of which carries direct operational consequence for workspaces that procure, specify, or ship in packaging across U.S. trade lanes.

**Force 1 — U.S. EPR legislation is accelerating across states.**

The Sustainable Packaging Coalition's living tracker (data current as of 1 January 2026) records enacted EPR packaging laws in California (SB 54, chaptered 30 June 2022), Colorado (HB 22-1355, passed May 2022), Illinois (SB 1555, passed July 2023), Connecticut (HB 6664, passed June 2023), Maryland (SB 222, passed May 2023; follow-on SB 901, passed May 2025), Minnesota (HF 3911, passed May 2024), and Washington (SB 5284, chaptered 17 May 2025). *Source: Sustainable Packaging Coalition, EPR Guide to All Policies, https://epr.sustainablepackaging.org/policies, data current as of 1 January 2026.*

Active introduced bills as of early 2026 cover Rhode Island (three bills introduced February 2026), Missouri, Georgia, New York, New Hampshire, and Wisconsin. *Source: Sustainable Packaging Coalition, EPR Guide to All Policies, https://epr.sustainablepackaging.org/policies.

**Force 2 — Novel circular materials (seaweed-based film) have crossed from lab to commercial scale.**

EcoEnclose reports that 15,087 lbs of TPSea™ resin from partner Sway were used in seaweed-based packaging at real commercial scale in 2025. *Source: EcoEnclose 2025 EcoImpact / Sustainability Report, https://www.ecoenclose.com/ecoimpact.

Sway (founded by Julia Marsh, CEO, and Matt Mayes, COO) was named a TIME Best Invention of 2025, won Fast Company's World-Changing Idea award, and took first place in the TOM FORD Plastic Innovation Prize. *Source: DesignWanted, "Designing with the ocean: Sway's rise in seaweed packaging," 31 January 2026, https://designwanted.com/sway-seaweed-packaging-design-innovation/.

TPSea™ is described as "the first thermoplastic pellet made from seaweed, designed to melt and flow like conventional plastic without losing strength or flexibility." *Source: DesignWanted, https://designwanted.com/sway-seaweed-packaging-design-innovation/.

**Force 3 — Tariffs, supply chain shocks, and EPR compliance costs created measurable sustainability backsliding in 2025.**

EcoEnclose's CEO commentary states directly: "Tariffs, layoffs, ongoing supply chain shocks, and general economic uncertainty created real constraints. Most brands we work with genuinely want to choose the most circular and sustainable solution, but had to prioritize cost stability and risk mitigation this year. This meant that—at times—we made decisions we didn't love. Supporting brands by shifting from paper to plastic when necessary. Lowering recycled content to meet cost targets." *Source: EcoEnclose 2025 EcoImpact / Sustainability Report, https://www.ecoenclose.com/ecoimpact.

---

# Who's Driving It and What They Want

**State regulators** are expanding EPR architecture to shift packaging end-of-life costs from municipalities to producers and importers. The mechanism in enacted laws is a Producer Responsibility Organization (PRO) through which covered entities pay into a collective recycling-infrastructure fund.

*Analytical inference:* The geographic spread — from a Pacific Coast origin (California 2022) to mid-Atlantic (Maryland), Midwest (Illinois, Minnesota), New England (Connecticut), and Pacific Northwest (Washington 2025) — indicates that EPR is transitioning from a coastal experiment to a near-national compliance baseline, creating a predictable pattern where brands operating across state lines face stacking obligations.

**Innovative material suppliers** (specifically Sway and EcoEnclose acting as a commercial launch partner) are driving seaweed-based film toward industrial viability. Manufacturing partners named in public reporting as part of Sway's ecosystem include EcoEnclose, Atlantic Packaging, and Charter Next Generation. *Source: DesignWanted, https://designwanted.com/sway-seaweed-packaging-design-innovation/.

Sway operates, per its public description, "as a collaborative ecosystem, involving scientists, engineers, designers, manufacturers, and farms across more than 30 countries" and reports supporting "over 100 acres of cultivation." *Source: DesignWanted, https://designwanted.com/sway-seaweed-packaging-design-innovation/.

**Brand procurement teams** — under combined pressure from EPR compliance costs, tariff-driven input cost increases, and client-facing sustainability commitments — are making real-time trade-offs between circularity and cost. *Analytical inference:* This dynamic creates an asymmetry: brands with earlier compliance infrastructure investments face lower marginal EPR costs and can sustain circular material choices; brands that deferred are now absorbing both compliance setup costs and material cost pressures simultaneously.

---

# Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**EPR conversion from signal to obligation:**

Washington SB 5284 was chaptered 17 May 2025. Maryland SB 901 was chaptered 13 May 2025. Both cover all packaging types plus paper products. *Source: Sustainable Packaging Coalition, EPR Guide to All Policies, https://epr.sustainablepackaging.org/policies.

For California, CalRecycle administers SB 54 with a 2032 target horizon; *Analytical inference:* permanent CalRecycle regulations noted as effective May 2026 in corroborating sources represent the next near-term compliance conversion point for California-facing supply chains (source-confirmed date; see New Sources Identified for CalRecycle citation).

*Analytical inference:* The density of introduced bills in early 2026 (Rhode Island, Missouri, Georgia, New Hampshire, Wisconsin, New York) suggests a 12–24 month window in which additional states could cross from introduced to enacted, potentially reaching 9–10 enacted state EPR laws by end of 2027. This is an estimate based on the 2022–2025 legislative cadence; no specific enactment dates are confirmed for the 2026 cohort.

**Seaweed film conversion from pilot to procurement option:**

15,087 lbs of TPSea™ ran at commercial scale in 2025 through EcoEnclose's film lines. *Source: EcoEnclose 2025 EcoImpact, https://www.ecoenclose.com/ecoimpact. This is a sourced commercial-scale milestone, not a pilot quantity.

*Analytical inference:* The conversion trigger for broader procurement access is achieving price parity with conventional LDPE film and resolving batch-to-batch variability — EcoEnclose's CEO explicitly identifies both as active challenges: "running groundbreaking, bio-based, and next-generation materials on lines designed decades ago for PP and LDPE. These materials behave differently. Different melt points. Different rigidity. Variability from batch to batch." *Source: EcoEnclose 2025 EcoImpact, https://www.ecoenclose.com/ecoimpact.

No public timeline for price parity has been stated by either Sway or EcoEnclose. Re-check window: EcoEnclose's annual EcoImpact report (expected Q4 2026/Q1 2027) will carry the next volume and variability data point.

---

# Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

**For workspaces procuring or specifying packaging on U.S. trade lanes:**

*Operational implication:* EPR compliance obligations fall on "producers" — the definition of which varies by state law and, for importers and freight forwarders, *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether the workspace's role as importer of record, shipper, or logistics intermediary triggers PRO registration obligations in any enacted state law requires state-by-state legal review.

*Analytical inference:* The cost structure emerging from enacted EPR laws follows a PRO-fee model. The EcoEnclose CEO describes compliance work as "costly, time-consuming, and confusing," with resources diverted from packaging innovation toward regulatory compliance. Specific per-unit fee schedules are set by each state PRO and are not yet publicly available in uniform form.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces moving goods into California, the May 2026 effective date of permanent CalRecycle regulations (flagged in corroborating sources — see New Sources Identified) is the nearest hard compliance trigger in any enacted state EPR law.

**For workspaces with sustainability commitments to clients:**

EcoEnclose's data shows that in 2025, 7,745,597 lbs of recycled paper and plastic were used and kept out of landfills, 41,201,007 lbs of GHG emissions were avoided, and 100,319,583 gallons of water were saved — metrics produced by applying Environmental Paper Network LCA assumptions to actual volume. *Source: EcoEnclose 2025 EcoImpact, https://www.ecoenclose.com/ecoimpact.

*Operational implication:* For workspaces that report Scope 3 packaging emissions or provide sustainability metrics to clients, the EcoEnclose EcoImpact methodology (Environmental Paper Network LCA) provides a traceable calculation basis — but the workspace should confirm it has reviewed the underlying LCA assumptions before citing these figures directly to clients.

*Operational implication:* The 77% PCR content custom pouch film represents the current high-water mark in publicly available recycled-content pouches from this supplier. EcoEnclose describes it as "one of the highest PCR percentages in the industry today." *Source: EcoEnclose 2025 EcoImpact, https://www.ecoenclose.com/ecoimpact. For cargo verticals that rely on pouches (cosmetics, food, pharmaceuticals), this is a relevant sourcing specification.

**For workspaces with air-primary and road-secondary transport modes:**

*Operational implication:* Lighter-weight packaging materials — including thin seaweed-based flexible film — reduce shipment weight and dimensional weight, with direct cost consequence on air freight. The magnitude depends on specific air carrier rate structures and is not publicly available; effect on air-freight cost requires carrier-specific data.

---

# Competitive Implications

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces that resolve EPR registration status and PRO enrollment early — in enacted states covering their packaging volumes — avoid the compliance-cost spike that EcoEnclose's client base absorbed in 2025. Early movers gain the resource capacity to invest in material innovation rather than retroactive compliance.

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces that can offer clients seaweed-based or high-PCR packaging options gain a demonstrable differentiation point in competitive pitches, particularly for cargo verticals (apparel, cosmetics, retail) where the named brand partners of Sway — J.Crew, Burton, Dr. Bronner's, Faherty, Florence — are active. These names appear in DesignWanted's reporting as part of Sway's brand network. *Source: DesignWanted, https://designwanted.com/sway-seaweed-packaging-design-innovation/.

*Analytical inference:* The supply constraint on TPSea™ at 15,087 lbs commercial-scale throughput in 2025 implies limited near-term availability for workspaces that were not already in EcoEnclose's EcoAlly network. Early engagement with this supply chain is a positioning action, not a purchasing action.

No named direct competitor positioning data for the workspace's freight-forwarding peer group was available in the source material. This gap is explicitly noted; competitive claims in this section are analytical.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

— U.S. state EPR packaging laws have now been enacted in seven states (California, Colorado, Illinois, Connecticut, Maryland, Minnesota, Washington) as of the 2025 legislative cycle, with additional states introducing legislation in early 2026. For clients shipping into any of these states, EPR compliance timelines are real and active. *Source: Sustainable Packaging Coalition, https://epr.sustainablepackaging.org/policies; EcoEnclose blog, https://ecoenclose.com/blog (EPR legislation resource hub cited therein).*

— Seaweed-based flexible film (Sway's TPSea™) has reached commercial-scale production through EcoEnclose's manufacturing lines, with 15,087 lbs processed in 2025. This is a sourced milestone, not a forecast. *Source: EcoEnclose 2025 EcoImpact, https://www.ecoenclose.com/ecoimpact.

— High-PCR content pouches at 77% post-consumer recycled content are commercially available from at least one supplier (EcoEnclose) — a useful benchmark when evaluating supplier sustainability claims. *Source: EcoEnclose 2025 EcoImpact, https://www.ecoenclose.com/ecoimpact.

— The 2025 macroeconomic environment (tariffs, supply chain shocks) demonstrably caused brands to make less circular packaging decisions. Clients who locked in sustainable packaging specifications before cost volatility hit are better positioned than those who deferred.

**Pitfalls to avoid:**

— Do not cite EcoEnclose's aggregate environmental savings figures (lbs of GHG avoided, gallons of water saved) as if they apply to the workspace's specific shipments. These are portfolio-level LCA estimates across EcoEnclose's full customer base, not shipment-level calculations. *Legal Confirmation Required:* using these figures in client-facing sustainability disclosures without tracing the specific LCA methodology and confirming its applicability to the workspace's actual packaging materials and volumes carries greenwashing and disclosure risk.

— Do not characterize compostable packaging as circular without qualification. EcoEnclose's own framework states: "For eCommerce Packaging, Designing for Composting is NOT Considered Circular" — because composting means materials are no longer available for re-entry into new goods. *Source: EcoEnclose Sustainable Packaging Framework, https://www.ecoenclose.com/our-sustainable-packaging-framework.

— Do not assert that any specific EPR law "applies to" the workspace without state-by-state legal review. *Legal Confirmation Required:* obligated party definitions vary by statute and the workspace's role (importer of record, freight forwarder, shipper) determines whether it is a covered producer in any given state.

---

# What the Workspace Should Do Now

**1. Map packaging volumes to enacted EPR states.**
Identify what packaging the workspace places on the market (or arranges to be placed) in California, Colorado, Illinois, Connecticut, Maryland, Minnesota, and Washington. This is prerequisite to any PRO registration assessment. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether the workspace's logistics role triggers producer obligations in each state requires legal review by counsel familiar with the relevant statutes.

**2. Flag California May 2026 as the nearest hard regulatory date.**
CalRecycle's permanent regulations under SB 54 are noted in corroborating sources as effective May 2026. For workspaces with California-bound cargo, this is the nearest actionable compliance trigger in the U.S. EPR landscape. *Legal Confirmation Required:* the specific obligations and covered-entity definitions under the permanent regulations require review of the CalRecycle regulatory text.

**3. Monitor the 2026 state legislative cohort.**
Rhode Island (three bills introduced February 2026), Missouri, Georgia, New Hampshire, Wisconsin, and New York all have active introduced bills as of the source data date. *Source: Sustainable Packaging Coalition, https://epr.sustainablepackaging.org/policies. Set a legislative tracker re-check for Q3 2026 to capture any enactments in the current session.

**4. Engage EcoEnclose's EcoAlly network if high-PCR or seaweed-film specifications are client requirements.**
TPSea™ supply at commercial scale ran through EcoEnclose's film lines in 2025. The EcoAlly relationship is the documented access point for this material. No other supply channel for TPSea™ flexible film is identified in the sourced material.

**5. Add EPR state mapping to new client onboarding conversations.**
For cargo verticals (apparel, cosmetics, food, subscription boxes, retail) where clients are placing packaging on the U.S. market, the EPR compliance question is now a standard due-diligence item. Raising it proactively positions the workspace as a compliance-aware logistics partner rather than a reactive one.

**6. Set monitoring re-check: EcoEnclose EcoImpact 2026 (expected Q4 2026 / Q1 2027)** for the next TPSea™ volume data point, variability resolution progress, and PCR content benchmarks. This is the primary forward data source for material innovation trajectory.

---
